briefs.topic: Investigator and Sponsor-Investigator Responsibilities under 21 CFR Part 312 (IND-phase clinical trials), including Form FDA 1572, informed consent (21 CFR 50), financial disclosure (21 CFR 54), IRB assurance (21 CFR 56), and delegation of authority — the core OSSICRO user path
briefs.summary: An "investigator" (21 CFR 312.3(b)) is the individual under whose immediate direction an investigational drug is administered or dispensed; a "sponsor-investigator" is one individual who BOTH initiates and conducts the investigation — the operative structure of every investigator-initiated trial (IIT/IST) and the OSSICRO core user. The investigator's duties are fixed by 21 CFR 312.60-312.69 (Subpart D) and are memorialized in the signed Form FDA 1572 (Statement of Investigator), which the investigator executes under 312.53(c). The sponsor-investigator is uniquely burdened because they simultaneously carry the full sponsor obligation set (Subpart D, 312.50-312.59, plus IND submission 312.20-312.23, safety reporting 312.32, and annual reports 312.33) AND the entire investigator obligation set — the two role-holders in a conventional trial collapse into one person. Non-delegable, human-judgment functions — obtaining informed consent from each subject (Part 50), IRB review and approval (Part 56), SAE causality assessment, and the legal accountability that attaches to the 1572 signature and the IND holder — cannot be automated; software may draft, check, route, and track, but a qualified human must own each. This is the crux of the OSSICRO legal frame: a physician legitimately holds both roles (312.3), and AI absorbs the coordination/paperwork burden that normally makes the sponsor-investigator role impractical, without disintermediating the accountable human (contrast 312.52, where sponsor obligations transfer only to a legally accountable CRO entity, never to software).

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — general responsibilities
obligations_or_content.detail: Conduct the study per the signed investigator statement (Form 1572), the investigational plan/protocol, and applicable regulations; protect the rights, safety, and welfare of subjects; control the investigational drug; obtain informed consent per Part 50; ensure IRB review per Part 56.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — control of the investigational drug
obligations_or_content.detail: Administer the drug only to subjects under the investigator's personal supervision or under the supervision of a subinvestigator responsible to the investigator; do not supply the investigational drug to any person not authorized under Part 312 to receive it.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — drug disposition records
obligations_or_content.detail: Maintain adequate records of drug disposition: dates, quantity, and use by subjects. On termination, unused supplies must be returned to the sponsor or otherwise disposed of per 312.59.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — case histories
obligations_or_content.detail: Prepare and maintain adequate and accurate case histories recording all observations and other data pertinent to the investigation; case history must include case report forms, supporting source documents, and documentation that informed consent was obtained prior to participation.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — record retention period
obligations_or_content.detail: Retain records for 2 years following the date a marketing application is approved for the indication; or, if no application is filed or the application is not approved, 2 years after the investigation is discontinued and FDA is notified.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — progress reports
obligations_or_content.detail: Furnish all reports to the sponsor, who is responsible for collecting and evaluating results and submitting annual reports to FDA.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — safety reports (SAE to sponsor)
obligations_or_content.detail: Immediately report to the sponsor any serious adverse event, whether or not considered drug related, including an assessment of whether there is a reasonable possibility the drug caused the event. Study endpoints per protocol are excepted unless there is a reasonable possibility of causation. Non-serious AEs reported per protocol timelines.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — final report
obligations_or_content.detail: Provide the sponsor an adequate report shortly after completion of the investigator's participation.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — financial disclosure to sponsor
obligations_or_content.detail: Furnish sufficient accurate financial information to allow the sponsor/applicant to submit complete Part 54 certification or disclosure; update that information if any relevant change occurs during the study and for 1 year after completion.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — assurance of IRB review
obligations_or_content.detail: Assure that an IRB complying with Part 56 will be responsible for initial and continuing review and approval; promptly report to the IRB all changes in research activity and all unanticipated problems involving risk to subjects or others; make no changes without prior IRB approval except where necessary to eliminate apparent immediate hazards.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — FDA inspection of records
obligations_or_content.detail: Upon request from any properly authorized FDA officer, at reasonable times, permit access to, and copying and verification of, any records or reports under 312.62. Subject identities may be withheld unless records of particular individuals require detailed study or there is reason to believe records do not represent actual results.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator — controlled substances
obligations_or_content.detail: If the investigational drug is a Schedule controlled substance, store it in a securely locked, substantially constructed cabinet or enclosure with access limited to prevent diversion; take precautions against theft or diversion.

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-Investigator — definition (dual role)
obligations_or_content.detail: An individual who both initiates and conducts an investigation, and under whose immediate direction the investigational drug is administered or dispensed. When an individual is a sponsor-investigator, the applicable Part 312 provisions covering BOTH sponsor and investigator apply.

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-Investigator — carries ALL sponsor duties additionally
obligations_or_content.detail: Beyond every investigator duty above, the sponsor-investigator must also discharge the sponsor obligations of Subpart D: general responsibilities (312.50), selecting qualified investigators / obtaining 1572s / CVs / financial disclosure (312.53 — here, of oneself), informing investigators via an investigator brochure and new safety findings (312.55), monitoring the investigation (312.56), sponsor recordkeeping and retention (312.57), permitting sponsor-record inspection (312.58), and disposition of unused supply (312.59).

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-Investigator — must hold the IND
obligations_or_content.detail: Must prepare and submit an IND (unless exempt) meeting 312.20/312.22/312.23 content-and-format requirements, receive the IND number, observe the 30-day safe-to-proceed waiting period before dosing, and act as the regulatory sponsor of record.

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-Investigator — IND safety reporting to FDA
obligations_or_content.detail: As sponsor, submit expedited IND safety reports: 7-calendar-day report for unexpected fatal or life-threatening suspected adverse reactions; 15-calendar-day IND safety report (Form FDA 3500A or narrative) for other serious and unexpected suspected adverse reactions, findings from other studies, and increased occurrence. This FDA-facing duty exists ONLY because of the sponsor role — a plain investigator only reports to the sponsor (312.64).

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-Investigator — annual report to FDA
obligations_or_content.detail: Submit a brief annual report to the IND within 60 days of the IND anniversary date (study progress, safety summary, protocol changes, etc.).

obligations_or_content.item: Form FDA 1572 — purpose and legal effect
obligations_or_content.detail: The Statement of Investigator; the investigator's signed, legally binding commitment required before participating in an IND study (312.53(c)). It is provided to the sponsor (in a sponsor-investigator IND, retained/held by that individual and referenced in the IND), NOT submitted directly to FDA by a site investigator. Signing without meeting the commitments is grounds for disqualification.

obligations_or_content.item: Form FDA 1572 — fields 1-8 (identifying content)
obligations_or_content.detail: (1) Name/address of investigator; (2) education, training, experience — CV or statement of qualifications; (3) name/address of research facility(ies); (4) name/address of clinical laboratory facilities; (5) name/address of the IRB responsible for review; (6) names of subinvestigators; (7) name and IND/protocol code number of each protocol; (8) phase of investigation and, as attachments, the protocol (Phase 2/3) or an outline (Phase 1).
obligations_or_content.citation: Form FDA 1572, Fields 1-8

obligations_or_content.item: Form FDA 1572 — Field 9 commitments the investigator signs
obligations_or_content.detail: By signing, the investigator commits to: conduct the study per the protocol and make changes only after notifying the sponsor, except to protect subjects; personally conduct or supervise the investigation; ensure all subjects are informed the drug is investigational and that informed consent (Part 50) and IRB review/approval (Part 56) requirements are met; report adverse experiences to the sponsor per 312.64; read and understand the investigator's brochure including potential risks and side effects; ensure associates/colleagues/employees assisting are informed of their obligations; maintain adequate and accurate records per 312.62 and make them available for inspection per 312.68; ensure a Part 56-compliant IRB conducts initial and continuing review and that changes/unanticipated problems are reported to it; comply with all other requirements of Part 312.

obligations_or_content.item: Informed consent — non-delegable subject-facing duty
obligations_or_content.detail: No investigator may involve a human as a subject unless legally effective informed consent is obtained; consent sought under circumstances giving the subject opportunity to decide without coercion; required elements (312.60 → Part 50): purpose, procedures, risks/discomforts, benefits, alternatives, confidentiality, compensation/injury provisions, contacts, voluntary-participation statement. Documented by signed consent form (50.27). This is a human judgment/relationship act OSSICRO drafts and version-controls but must never execute.

obligations_or_content.item: Financial disclosure — Part 54 certification vs disclosure
obligations_or_content.detail: For each covered clinical study, the applicant/sponsor files either Form FDA 3454 (certification that no covered financial arrangements exist) or Form FDA 3455 (disclosure of the arrangement plus steps taken to minimize bias). Covered arrangements: compensation tied to study outcome; significant equity interest > $50,000 in a publicly traded sponsor (or ANY equity in a non-publicly-traded sponsor whose value cannot be readily determined); significant payments of other sorts > $25,000 (grants, equipment, retainers, honoraria) exclusive of study costs; proprietary interest in the tested product. Reporting window covers the study period plus 1 year after completion. In a sponsor-investigator IND the individual must certify/disclose their OWN interests.

obligations_or_content.item: IRB assurance — non-delegable ethics judgment
obligations_or_content.detail: A Part 56-compliant IRB must give initial and continuing review and approval; the IRB's risk/benefit determination and approval decision is an independent human/committee judgment that cannot be automated or substituted. Investigator may not enroll subjects before IRB approval and may not deviate without IRB approval except to eliminate immediate hazards (then report promptly).

obligations_or_content.item: Delegation of authority — what CAN and CANNOT be delegated
obligations_or_content.detail: The investigator may delegate specific study tasks to qualified subinvestigators/staff and must maintain a signed, dated delegation-of-authority log with training records (ICH E6(R2/R3) 4.2/2.x; FDA Investigator Responsibilities guidance). Non-delegable: overall supervision and the acts tied to the 1572 signature (protocol compliance, subject protection, records integrity). Delegating a task never transfers the investigator's regulatory accountability. Contrast 312.52: a SPONSOR may transfer obligations only to a legally accountable CRO (in writing); software is not a permissible transferee.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.3(b), 312.52; FDA Guidance 'Investigator Responsibilities' (2009); ICH E6(R3) §2/§3

non_delegable_or_gating: Obtaining informed consent from each human subject (Part 50) — a human relational/judgment act; software drafts and version-controls the ICF but a qualified investigator/designee must obtain it
IRB review and approval decision (Part 56) — independent committee judgment; cannot be automated or bypassed; enrollment is gated on it
SAE seriousness and causality/expectedness assessment (312.64(b), 312.32) — clinical judgment feeding expedited reporting; OSSICRO may flag/route/pre-populate but not adjudicate
The Form FDA 1572 signature and its Field 9 commitments (312.53(c)) — a personal legal attestation that binds the human investigator
Holding and legal accountability for the IND (312.20-312.23) and all sponsor decisions — cannot vest in software; must be a legally accountable person/entity (312.52)
Overall supervision of the investigation and protocol-compliance decisions — delegable tasks yes, accountability no
Financial certification/disclosure attestation (Forms 3454/3455) — a signed representation to FDA
Any money movement, submission-to-FDA, or send-as-a-named-person action — must be gated to explicit human authorization (mirrors OSSICRO hard line and Constitution §7)

briefs.topic: The CRO role, 21 CFR 312.52 transfer of obligations, CRO functional/margin breakdown, and the coordination-vs-legally-accountable line for OSSICRO's thin "micro-CRO"
briefs.summary: A Contract Research Organization (CRO) is an entity to which a drug sponsor may, in writing, transfer any or all of the sponsor's regulatory obligations under 21 CFR Part 312; upon assumption, the CRO becomes directly liable to FDA "to the same extent as a sponsor" for those obligations (§312.52(b)). This is the load-bearing legal fact for OSSICRO: obligations transfer only to a legally accountable *entity*, never to software — so pure software disintermediation of the CRO is structurally impossible, while software absorbing the *coordination labor* is not. CRO work decomposes into ~8 functional streams (start-up/site activation, regulatory submissions support, clinical monitoring/CRA, project management, pharmacovigilance/safety, data management, biostatistics, medical writing); ~90-95% of a CRO's direct cost is labor, gross margins run ~40-50%, and the marked-up service margin is ~15-25% on direct cost — margin sits in the *labor-coordination* layers, exactly the automatable surface. The functions that legally require an accountable human/entity (investigator conduct, informed-consent supervision, SAE causality/expedited reporting decisions, IRB judgment, sponsor recordkeeping/QA sign-off, the QP/PI signature) are the irreducible "micro-CRO." The failed pure-disintermediation plays — Science 37 (decentralized-trial software, ~$1B SPAC valuation collapsing to a ~$38M take-private by eMed) and TrialSpark→Formation Bio (abandoned the CRO service model to become an AI-native drug *developer*) — both demonstrate that software-only trial disintermediation does not sustain as a business; value accrues to whoever holds the asset/accountability, not the coordination tooling. The viable OSSICRO path is the sponsor-investigator IND (§312.3), where one physician lawfully holds both roles and AI absorbs the paperwork that normally makes that impractical.

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor may transfer any or all Part 312 obligations to a CRO, but only in writing
obligations_or_content.detail: 'A sponsor may transfer responsibility for any or all of the obligations set forth in this part to a contract research organization. Any such transfer shall be described in writing.' If not all obligations are transferred, the writing must describe each obligation the CRO assumes; a general 'all obligations transferred' statement suffices only for a total transfer. Critically: 'Any obligation not covered by the written description shall be deemed not to have been transferred.' The transfer instrument is the operative legal document (typically the Transfer of Regulatory Obligations, TORO, appended to the Master Services Agreement / task order).

obligations_or_content.item: A CRO that assumes an obligation is directly liable to FDA as if it were the sponsor
obligations_or_content.detail: 'A contract research organization that assumes any obligation of a sponsor shall comply with the specific regulations in this chapter applicable to this obligation and shall be subject to the same regulatory action as a sponsor for failure to comply... all references to "sponsor" in this part apply to a contract research organization to the extent that it assumes one or more obligations of the sponsor.' This is why the transferee must be a legally accountable entity — FDA enforcement (Warning Letters, injunctions, IND clinical hold, debarment) runs against the CRO directly for assumed obligations. Software cannot be a §312.52(b) transferee because it cannot 'be subject to the same regulatory action as a sponsor.'

obligations_or_content.item: The sponsor obligations that are the transferable menu (Subpart D, §312.50–312.59)
obligations_or_content.detail: §312.50 general responsibilities (select qualified investigators, ship drug only to participating investigators, ensure proper monitoring, ensure IND/protocol compliance, maintain effective IND, ensure FDA and investigators informed of significant new adverse effects/risks); §312.53 selecting investigators and monitors (obtain FDA-1572, CV, financial disclosure, signed protocol/investigator agreement); §312.55 informing investigators (Investigator's Brochure, new safety information); §312.56 review of ongoing investigations (monitor progress, ensure monitoring, discontinue non-compliant investigators, review safety); §312.57 recordkeeping/retention (disposition records, financial-interest records, 2-year retention rule); §312.58 inspection of sponsor records; §312.59 disposition of unused drug. Any of these can be assigned to a CRO under §312.52.

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator obligations are NOT transferred to a CRO — they belong to the clinical investigator
obligations_or_content.detail: Subpart D §312.60–312.70 bind the investigator personally: §312.60 general responsibilities (conduct per protocol, protect subject rights/safety, control of drug, obtain informed consent per Part 50, ensure IRB review per Part 56); §312.61 control of investigational drug; §312.62 investigator recordkeeping (case histories, drug accountability); §312.64 investigator reports (progress, safety — report SAEs to sponsor immediately); §312.66 assurance of IRB review; §312.68 inspection. A CRO can supply CRAs to *monitor* investigator compliance but cannot *assume* the investigator's conduct obligations — those are non-transferable to a service vendor. This is the accountability floor OSSICRO must route to a licensed human.

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-investigator dual role — the OSSICRO-viable legal structure
obligations_or_content.detail: 'Sponsor-Investigator means an individual who both initiates and conducts an investigation, and under whose immediate direction the investigational drug is administered or dispensed. The term does not include any person other than an individual (i.e., it does not include a corporation or agency).' Per §312.3(b): 'The obligations of a sponsor-investigator... include both those of a sponsor and those of an investigator.' A single physician therefore lawfully holds both role-sets in an investigator-initiated IND — no illegal disintermediation occurs because no obligation is transferred to a non-entity. AI absorbs the coordination burden that normally makes this impractical for a solo clinician. FDA's own guidance ('Overview of Sponsor-Investigator Roles and Responsibilities,' FDA media 174660) codifies this pathway.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.3(b) (definitions); FDA, 'Overview of Sponsor-Investigator Roles and Responsibilities' (fda.gov/media/174660/download)

obligations_or_content.item: Functional decomposition of CRO services (the 8 streams) — and the coordination-vs-accountable classification
obligations_or_content.detail: (1) Start-up / SITE ACTIVATION: feasibility, site selection, contracts/budgets, regulatory-document collection (1572s, CVs, financial disclosure, IRB approvals), essential-document/TMF assembly — MOSTLY COORDINATION LABOR (automatable/templatable); accountable residue = the sponsor QA sign-off. (2) REGULATORY SUBMISSIONS SUPPORT: IND assembly (Form FDA 1571), eCTD publishing, annual reports, safety reports — COORDINATION-heavy drafting, but the 1571 signature and IND-holder responsibility are non-delegable to software. (3) MONITORING / CRA: source-data verification, protocol-compliance review, on-site/remote visits — HYBRID; the *judgment* (is this a reportable deviation?) requires a qualified monitor; scheduling/tracking/report-drafting is automatable. (4) PROJECT MANAGEMENT: timelines, vendor coordination, status reporting — PURE COORDINATION (automatable). (5) PHARMACOVIGILANCE / SAFETY: SAE intake, MedDRA coding, narrative writing, expedited (7/15-day IND safety report) submissions, aggregate reports — INTAKE/CODING/NARRATIVE-DRAFT automatable; CAUSALITY and EXPECTEDNESS determination and the medical-monitor sign-off are NON-DELEGABLE. (6) DATA MANAGEMENT: EDC build, CRF design, edit checks, query management, database lock — HIGHLY automatable coordination. (7) BIOSTATISTICS: SAP, randomization, TLFs, analysis — automatable computation, but statistician sign-off on the SAP/CSR is accountable. (8) MEDICAL WRITING: protocol, IB, ICF, CSR — DRAFTING automatable; qualified-author/sponsor approval accountable.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR Part 312 Subparts B & D; ICH E6(R3) GCP; Form FDA 1571 (IND), Form FDA 1572 (Statement of Investigator); ICH E2A (expedited safety reporting); 21 CFR 312.32 (IND safety reports)

obligations_or_content.item: Where CRO margin actually sits — and why it is exactly the automatable surface
obligations_or_content.detail: CRO gross margins run ~40–50% industry-wide; roughly 90–95% of a CRO's *direct* cost is labor (PRA disclosed 95.7% of direct cost was employee/staffing-agency labor in 2015); CROs typically add a ~15–25% margin markup on top of pass-through direct costs (negotiable). Full-Service Outsourcing (FSO) carries mid-to-high-teens EBITDA vs high-single/low-teens for Functional Service Provider (FSP) work. The margin is therefore concentrated in the *billable-hour coordination layers* — project management, monitoring hours, data management, medical writing — precisely the functions with the least irreducible legal accountability. This is the OSSICRO thesis's economic core: automate the labor that generates the margin, retain a thin accountable spine.
obligations_or_content.citation: CRO financial disclosures (PRA Health Sciences direct-cost breakdown); industry margin analyses (Quanticate, IntuitionLabs CRO cost guide, Healthcare IB CRO business model)

obligations_or_content.item: The irreducible 'micro-CRO' — functions that legally require an accountable entity/human, which OSSICRO must GATE and route, never own
obligations_or_content.detail: Non-delegable to software (must be a licensed human or accountable legal entity): (a) informed consent of the patient — obtained by the investigator, Part 50, cannot be automated; (b) IRB/ethics judgment — Part 56, an independent constituted board; (c) SAE causality and expectedness determination and the medical-monitor safety judgment — a qualified physician; (d) the investigator's conduct-of-trial obligations (§312.60); (e) the sponsor's IND-holder signature on Form FDA 1571 and the Statement of Investigator Form FDA 1572; (f) QA release / regulatory sign-off and the person legally 'subject to the same regulatory action' under §312.52(b); (g) in EU/ICH contexts, the Qualified Person (QP) for pharmacovigilance / QPPV and IMP release. The thin micro-CRO is: a named accountable legal entity (or the sponsor-investigator personally) plus the qualified humans who own (a)-(g). Everything else is open-source, templatable, physician-operable software.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR Part 50; 21 CFR Part 56; 21 CFR 312.32 & 312.64(b) (SAE reporting); 21 CFR 312.52(b) (accountable-entity requirement); ICH E6(R3) sponsor/investigator sections; EU CTR 536/2014 & GVP Module I (QPPV) for EU frame

obligations_or_content.item: Failed pure-disintermediation play #1 — Science 37
obligations_or_content.detail: Science 37 built decentralized-clinical-trial (DCT) software + a metasite/telehealth network and rode COVID-era DCT hype + a 2021 SPAC merger to a >$1B valuation. Revenue growth then stalled (Q3 net bookings collapsed to ~$4.7M vs $35.9M a year prior; canceled COVID studies; longer sales cycles), the stock fell below Nasdaq's $1.00 continued-listing minimum (Listing Rule 5450(a)(1)) triggering a delisting deficiency notice, and in Jan 2024 it went private with diagnostics startup eMed at ~$38M — a fraction of peak. Lesson for OSSICRO: selling trial-coordination software as the product, without holding the asset or the accountable role, is a thin, cyclical, low-moat business; the coordination layer alone does not capture durable value.
obligations_or_content.citation: Science 37 Form 8-K (Notice of Delisting, Nasdaq Rule 5450(a)(1), Jun 2023); Endpoints News / MedCity News coverage of eMed take-private (Jan 2024); Fierce Biotech earnings coverage

obligations_or_content.item: Failed pure-disintermediation play #2 — TrialSpark → Formation Bio
obligations_or_content.detail: TrialSpark (founded 2016) began as trial-coordination software, expanded into a full-service tech-enabled CRO running end-to-end trials on its platform, then concluded that the durable value was in the *asset*, not the service: from 2022 it began acquiring/in-licensing and developing its own drug assets, and in Dec 2023 rebranded to Formation Bio to signal the shift to an AI-native drug *developer* (subsequently raising a Sanofi-backed $372M Series D to license more drugs). The company that best knew the economics of tech-enabled CRO work voted with its feet: it abandoned the CRO service model to become a sponsor/developer. Lesson for OSSICRO: 'be the CRO via software' was not where the founders saw sustainable margin — reinforcing that the coordination layer is a cost-to-automate, and accountability/asset ownership is where value concentrates. OSSICRO's answer is not to *become* pharma but to hand the sponsor-investigator physician the automation so they hold the accountable role themselves.
obligations_or_content.citation: TrialSpark→Formation Bio rebrand (PR Newswire, Dec 2023); Contrary Research company report; Fierce Biotech ($372M Series D, Sanofi-backed)

obligations_or_content.item: Confirmed vs interpretive positions (calibration for the wiki)
obligations_or_content.detail: CONFIRMED (black-letter): §312.52 written-transfer + CRO direct-liability; the sponsor/investigator/sponsor-investigator obligation sets in Part 312 Subpart D and §312.3; informed consent (Part 50) and IRB (Part 56) as non-delegable; Form FDA 1571/1572 signatures as accountable acts. INTERPRETIVE / OSSICRO-thesis positions (label as such): (1) that a software package can lawfully perform all *coordination* labor so long as every accountable act is gated to a qualified human — supportable but not a settled FDA position on AI-generated regulatory documents; (2) the precise boundary of what monitoring judgment can be software-assisted vs must be human — ICH E6(R3) risk-based/centralized monitoring opens room but does not eliminate the qualified-monitor requirement; (3) whether AI-drafted CSR/protocol/ICF text satisfies 'qualified medical writer' expectations — currently a human-sign-off question, not an automation-permitted one. The wiki must flag every place OSSICRO's automation touches an accountable act and show the human gate.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.52, 312.3, Part 50, Part 56; ICH E6(R3) (risk-based/centralized monitoring); FDA draft guidance on AI in drug development (evolving — cite as interpretive)

briefs.topic: Pharma Sponsor Perspective on Early-Phase Trials and External Investigators (for OSSICRO)
briefs.summary: This brief maps how a pharmaceutical sponsor views early-phase (Phase 1/2) development and its interface with external clinicians, from the sponsor's own vantage. It distinguishes four fundamentally different channels by which a physician outside the company can obtain a pharma investigational product: (1) enrolling a patient as a site in the sponsor's own protocol; (2) requesting drug + support for the physician's own study via an Investigator-Initiated / Investigator-Sponsored Study (IIS/IST) program, in which the physician becomes a sponsor-investigator holding their own IND; (3) individual-patient or expanded-access treatment use under 21 CFR Part 312 Subpart I (312.305/312.310/312.315/312.320) — which is treatment, not research, and is legally and operationally distinct from a trial; and (4) medical-affairs scientific exchange, which is walled off from commercial promotion. The controlling constraint throughout is that a pharma sponsor will only transfer product, data, and money to a physician who can carry the corresponding legal accountability (GCP-qualified investigator per 21 CFR 312.53/Form FDA 1572, financial disclosure per 21 CFR Part 54, FMV-set compensation defensible under the Anti-Kickback Statute, and Sunshine Act/Open Payments reportability). For OSSICRO the load-bearing insight is that pharma's trust gate is not software-shaped — it is entity-shaped: sponsors need a legally-accountable investigator/site with real GCP infrastructure, and OSSICRO's value is absorbing the coordination burden that makes a small/new sponsor-investigator site fundable and activatable, not replacing the accountable human.

obligations_or_content.item: How pharma runs Phase 1/2 (sponsor operating model)
obligations_or_content.detail: A pharma sponsor operating an early-phase program owns the IND (21 CFR 312.23), the protocol, the Investigator's Brochure (21 CFR 312.55), drug supply/labeling (312.6), safety reporting/IND safety reports (312.32), monitoring (312.50-312.56), and record retention. Phase 1 (312.21(a)) is small (~20-100 subjects), safety/PK/dose-ranging, often single or few specialized units (Phase 1 units / dedicated CRUs), frequently healthy volunteers or, in oncology/neuro, patients. Phase 2 (312.21(b)) is dose-finding + preliminary efficacy in the target indication across a modest number of selected sites. The sponsor typically delegates operational conduct to a CRO under 21 CFR 312.52 (transfer of obligations, documented in writing) but retains ultimate sponsor accountability. Governance layers a sponsor stands up around an early-phase asset: a DSMB/DMC for unblinded safety review (FDA guidance 'Establishment and Operation of Clinical Trial Data Monitoring Committees', 2006), a medical monitor, and pharmacovigilance intake for expedited reporting.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.21, 312.23, 312.32, 312.50-312.56, 312.52, 312.55; FDA Guidance 'Establishment and Operation of Clinical Trial Data Monitoring Committees' (2006)

obligations_or_content.item: Site selection and feasibility (what the sponsor evaluates before choosing a site)
obligations_or_content.detail: Sponsor/CRO issues a Confidential Disclosure Agreement (CDA) then a feasibility questionnaire assessing: patient population/access to the indication, PI experience and prior GCP trial history, coordinator/staff bandwidth, competing trials, pharmacy and drug-storage/temperature-controlled capacity, lab/imaging capabilities, IRB arrangement (central vs local), data-management/EDC readiness, and regulatory inspection history (Form FDA 483s / warning letters). A Site Qualification Visit (SQV) confirms the questionnaire on-site. Sponsors weight enrollment realism heavily because non-enrolling ('zero-enroller') sites are the dominant cost sink in early-phase programs. This is precisely the stage where a small/new site is at a structural disadvantage — no track record — which OSSICRO must address with verifiable qualification evidence rather than assertions.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) Good Clinical Practice (adopted Jan 2025; FDA guidance media/169090) sponsor responsibilities on investigator/site selection; standard industry feasibility/SQV practice

obligations_or_content.item: What pharma needs from a site to ACTIVATE it (essential documents / regulatory green-light package)
obligations_or_content.detail: Site activation ('green light' to ship drug and screen) requires a complete essential-document package: executed Clinical Trial Agreement + budget; IRB/IEC approval of protocol and ICF; Form FDA 1572 (Statement of Investigator) signed by the PI committing to conduct per protocol and GCP; PI and sub-I current CVs and medical licenses; financial disclosure forms (21 CFR Part 54; Forms FDA 3454/3455); GCP and protocol-specific training records; signed protocol signature/agreement page; lab certifications (CLIA/CAP) and normal ranges; delegation-of-authority log; drug-accountability and storage SOPs; and IP/pharmacy setup. Only after these are filed and reconciled does the sponsor hold a Site Initiation Visit (SIV) to train the team and release drug. Under ICH E6(R3) these are framed as risk-proportionate 'essential records' retained in the Trial Master File (TMF)/Investigator Site File.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.53(c) (Form FDA 1572 / investigator commitments); 21 CFR Part 54 & Forms FDA 3454/3455 (financial disclosure); ICH E6(R3) Essential Records / TMF; FDA Compliance Program 7348.811 (BIMO site inspections)

obligations_or_content.item: Form FDA 1572 — the investigator's legal commitment
obligations_or_content.detail: The 1572 is the sponsor's core trust instrument for an external investigator: by signing, the PI legally commits to conduct the study per protocol, personally supervise, ensure IRB review, report adverse events, maintain records, and permit FDA inspection. It names the site, sub-investigators, IRB, and facilities. A sponsor cannot ship investigational drug to an investigator who has not filed a 1572. The 1572 is a commitment to the SPONSOR (not filed directly with FDA by the site); the sponsor collects and retains it. For a sponsor-investigator, the same person's obligations are captured differently (they hold the IND directly), but the substantive GCP commitments are identical.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.53(c)(1); Form FDA 1572; FDA Guidance 'Frequently Asked Questions — Statement of Investigator (Form FDA 1572)' (2010)

obligations_or_content.item: CTMS / sponsor portals / site-facing systems
obligations_or_content.detail: Sponsors run the trial on a stack the external site must interoperate with: a CTMS (Clinical Trial Management System — e.g., Veeva Vault CTMS, Medidata) for site status/milestones/monitoring; an eTMF for essential documents; an EDC (Electronic Data Capture — e.g., Medidata Rave, Veeva Vault EDC, Oracle) for CRF data; an IRT/IWRS/RTSM for randomization and drug supply; a safety database (e.g., Argus, ArisGlobal) for pharmacovigilance; and increasingly a Site/Investigator PORTAL (Veeva SiteVault, IIS/grants portals) as the single door for document exchange, e-signature, payments, and queries. For IIS specifically, nearly every large pharma runs a public web PORTAL for concept/proposal submission (Genentech, Roche, LEO, Acadia programs). OSSICRO's 'pharma-style frontend' must speak these systems' document conventions (21 CFR Part 11 compliant e-records/e-signatures, audit trails, validated/access-controlled per ICH E6(R3)).
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR Part 11 (electronic records/signatures); ICH E6(R3) computerized-systems expectations; sponsor IIS portals (gene.com, roche.com, leo-pharma.com, acadia.com)

obligations_or_content.item: Investigator-Initiated / Investigator-Sponsored Studies (IIS/IST) — how a physician requests drug + support
obligations_or_content.detail: IIS/IST is the sanctioned channel for a physician to get pharma product and/or funding for THEIR OWN study. Mechanics: the physician submits a concept/synopsis through the company's IIS portal to a Medical Affairs-run review committee (scientific merit, strategic alignment, safety, budget). If approved, the company provides some combination of investigational or marketed drug supply, funding (a grant/contract), and sometimes assay/data support — but the physician/institution becomes the SPONSOR-INVESTIGATOR: they conceive and write the protocol, hold the IND if one is required (21 CFR 312.3 allows one person to be both sponsor and investigator), obtain IRB approval, run pharmacovigilance, and own the data and publication. The pharma company is explicitly NOT the trial sponsor — it is a supporter/funder. This is the crux for OSSICRO path (b): the physician assumes BOTH sponsor and investigator regulatory obligations, and OSSICRO's role is to make that dual burden tractable. Key sponsor-investigator obligations that do not disappear: IND maintenance (312.23), annual reports (312.33), IND safety reports (312.32), protocol amendments (312.30), monitoring/records, and FDA/IRB correspondence.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.3 (definitions of sponsor, investigator, sponsor-investigator); 21 CFR 312.32/312.33 (safety + annual reports); industry IIS program pages (Roche, Genentech, LEO, Acadia)

obligations_or_content.item: Expanded Access / Compassionate Use — 21 CFR Part 312 Subpart I (DISTINCT from a trial)
obligations_or_content.detail: Expanded access is TREATMENT, not research: 'use of an investigational drug when the primary purpose is to diagnose, monitor, or treat a patient rather than to obtain the kind of information generally derived from clinical trials.' It is NOT a clinical trial — no efficacy endpoint, no randomization, and the 'investigator' label does not mean trial participation. Three tiers: (1) INDIVIDUAL patient / emergency use — 21 CFR 312.310, requested by the treating physician, typically via Form FDA 3926 (or 1571/1572 route), with emergency telephone authorization allowed and a 15-working-day follow-up submission; (2) INTERMEDIATE-size patient population — 21 CFR 312.315; (3) WIDESPREAD treatment IND/protocol — 21 CFR 312.320. General criteria for all (312.305): serious/immediately life-threatening disease, no comparable/satisfactory alternative therapy, potential benefit justifies risk, and provision won't interfere with clinical investigations. Critically, the drug's manufacturer MUST AGREE to provide the drug — FDA authorization does not compel a company to supply it, and companies maintain expanded-access policies (required for certain products under the 21st Century Cures Act, FDCA §561A). For OSSICRO, expanded access is the fastest path to get a named patient a drug but yields no trial data and needs manufacturer sign-off; it must be surfaced as a separate workflow from trial enrollment and from a sponsor-investigator IND.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.305, 312.310, 312.315, 312.320; Form FDA 3926; FDCA §561/§561A (21st Century Cures expanded-access policy requirement); FDA Guidance 'Expanded Access to Investigational Drugs for Treatment Use — Qs & As' (media/162793)

obligations_or_content.item: Expanded access vs. IIS vs. site-enrollment — the three-way distinction OSSICRO must never blur
obligations_or_content.detail: (A) Enroll patient in pharma's trial: pharma is sponsor, physician is a site investigator on an existing protocol, patient gets drug as a research subject, data flows to pharma; requires site activation package + 1572. (B) Sponsor-investigator IIS: physician designs and runs their OWN study, holds the IND, pharma only supplies drug/funding; physician owns data; requires full sponsor+investigator regulatory load. (C) Expanded access (312.310 etc.): physician treats ONE named patient (or a defined population) outside any trial; purpose is treatment not knowledge; manufacturer must agree to supply; minimal-to-no generalizable data. The guiding OSSICRO scenario ('clinician has a patient, wants a treatment') can resolve to ANY of the three depending on whether the patient fits an open protocol (A), whether a study is warranted (B), or whether no trial exists and it is single-patient treatment (C) — the engine must triage to the correct pathway, because the document sets, accountable parties, and legal exposure differ entirely.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.3, 312.52, 312.53, Subpart I (312.305-312.320); FDA Guidance 'Expanded Access... Qs & As'

obligations_or_content.item: Medical Affairs vs. Clinical Development (which pharma function OSSICRO's counterparty is)
obligations_or_content.detail: Clinical Development owns the company's OWN interventional trials (protocol, sites, IND) — counterparty for path (A) site enrollment. Medical Affairs owns non-promotional scientific exchange, MSLs (Medical Science Liaisons), and — importantly — the IIS/IST program and much of expanded-access adjudication — counterparty for paths (B) and (C). A compliance FIREWALL separates both from Commercial/Sales: MSLs may respond to unsolicited scientific inquiries and route IIS concepts, but cannot promote off-label use, cannot tie support to prescribing, and their metrics are not sales-based. For OSSICRO this means the physician's inbound request routes to Medical Affairs (grants/IIS or medical information), never to a sales rep, and the interaction must stay non-promotional and documented.
obligations_or_content.citation: PhRMA Code on Interactions with Health Care Professionals; FDCA §502 (promotional/off-label constraints); industry medical-affairs firewall practice

obligations_or_content.item: Contracting — CTA, budget, Fair Market Value
obligations_or_content.detail: The Clinical Trial Agreement (CTA) between sponsor and institution/investigator governs scope, indemnification, IP, publication rights, subject-injury coverage, data ownership, and payment. The BUDGET itemizes per-visit/per-procedure costs, coordinator/PI effort, overhead (typically 25-30% institutional indirect), IRB fees, screen-fail and start-up fees, and pass-throughs. Every payment must be set at FAIR MARKET VALUE (FMV) — compensation for bona fide services (physician time, procedures) at a defensible market rate, set in advance, and NOT tied to enrollment volume or referral. FMV is the operative defense under the Anti-Kickback Statute. For an IIS, the instrument is a grant/support agreement, and FMV plus documented scientific-merit approval is what keeps drug/funding from looking like an inducement.
obligations_or_content.citation: AKS personal-services / management-contract safe harbor 42 CFR 1001.952(d); FMV requirement (compensation set in advance, consistent with FMV, not volume/value-based); standard CTA structure

obligations_or_content.item: Anti-Kickback Statute (AKS) constraint
obligations_or_content.detail: 42 U.S.C. §1320a-7b(b) makes it a felony to knowingly offer/pay/solicit/receive remuneration to induce referrals or purchasing of items/services reimbursable by a federal healthcare program. Applied to trials: paying an investigator ABOVE FMV, or tying payment to enrollment/prescribing, is a red flag because investigators generate downstream drug utilization. Compliance relies on the personal-services safe harbor: written agreement, ≥1-year term, aggregate compensation set in advance at FMV, not determined by volume/value of referrals. OSSICRO-generated budgets and IIS support agreements must be structured to fit this — the software can template FMV-defensible structures but the FMV determination and the anti-inducement judgment are human/counsel functions to be surfaced and gated.
obligations_or_content.citation: 42 U.S.C. §1320a-7b(b); safe harbor 42 CFR 1001.952(d); HHS-OIG guidance

obligations_or_content.item: Physician Payments Sunshine Act / Open Payments (transparency)
obligations_or_content.detail: The Sunshine Act (ACA §6002; 42 CFR Part 403 Subpart I, 403.900-403.914) requires 'applicable manufacturers' to report annually to CMS virtually all payments/transfers of value to physicians and teaching hospitals — published on the public Open Payments database. Research payments (trial and IIS funding under a written agreement/protocol) are a distinct reportable category with a delayed-publication provision for pre-approval product research. Implication for OSSICRO/physician: every dollar of drug value, grant, or service payment a pharma partner provides to the physician is reportable and public. The engine should make counterparties' reporting obligations explicit and keep the physician's received-value record reconcilable, but the reporting duty sits with the manufacturer.
obligations_or_content.citation: ACA §6002; 42 CFR 403.900-403.914 (Open Payments); CMS Open Payments program

obligations_or_content.item: Financial disclosure of investigators (21 CFR Part 54)
obligations_or_content.detail: Sponsors must collect financial-interest information from all investigators (and sub-investigators) to certify absence of, or disclose, disqualifying financial arrangements when a marketing application is filed — via Form FDA 3454 (certification) or 3455 (disclosure). A sponsor-investigator running an IIS still faces this at any future application stage. OSSICRO should treat 3454/3455 collection as a standing part of the activation/onboarding document set.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR Part 54 (54.4); Forms FDA 3454/3455

obligations_or_content.item: What a pharma needs from OSSICRO/a small or new site to trust it
obligations_or_content.detail: A sponsor's trust gate for an unproven site is evidence of legally-accountable GCP capacity, not software polish. Concretely a pharma partner (or an IIS review committee) will want: (1) a qualified, licensed PI with a clean regulatory record and a signed 1572-equivalent commitment; (2) IRB/IEC of record (a central IRB relationship de-risks a new site fast); (3) demonstrable GCP training (ICH E6(R3)) for all delegated staff; (4) SOPs for informed consent, drug accountability/storage (temperature-monitored), source documentation, and AE/SAE reporting; (5) financial disclosures (Part 54) and an FMV-set budget; (6) a validated, Part 11-compliant document/data environment with audit trails and access control; (7) pharmacovigilance intake capable of meeting expedited-reporting timelines; (8) inspection-readiness (BIMO). OSSICRO's differentiator is that it can generate, check, validate, and version-control this entire package to sponsor standard — turning a new site's biggest liability (no track record, no coordinator army) into a verifiable, auditable dossier — while the non-delegable judgments (PI qualification attestation, IRB approval, consent, causality) remain human and gated. The single most persuasive artifact to a skeptical sponsor is a complete, internally-consistent essential-documents package plus a credentialed accountable investigator.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) sponsor site-selection/oversight + investigator qualification; 21 CFR 312.53, Part 54, Part 11; FDA BIMO Compliance Program 7348.811

obligations_or_content.item: Non-delegable / gating functions OSSICRO must surface, never automate
obligations_or_content.detail: Consistent with 21 CFR 312.52 (obligations transfer only to a legally accountable entity, not software): informed consent of the patient (21 CFR Part 50) is the investigator's act; IRB review and approval judgment (21 CFR Part 56) is the board's; SAE/causality/expectedness determination and IND safety reporting decisions (21 CFR 312.32) are the sponsor's/medical monitor's; the PI's 1572 attestation of qualification; FMV determination and anti-kickback judgment; and the manufacturer's decision to supply drug for expanded access. OSSICRO drafts, checks, routes, and tracks; a qualified human owns and signs each of these.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.52, Part 50 (informed consent), Part 56 (IRB), 312.32 (safety reporting)

briefs.topic: Coordination entities in early-phase clinical trials: IRB/IEC review, DSMB/DMC, clinical trial monitoring, pharmacovigilance/safety reporting, and the ICH E6(R3) essential-records/TMF layer — entities, workflows, and inter-entity document flows for OSSICRO
briefs.summary: Four oversight/coordination bodies sit around a sponsor-investigator IND and must be fed, timed, and inter-connected: (1) the IRB/IEC, the human-subjects gatekeeper governed by 21 CFR 56 (and 21 CFR 50 for consent) for FDA-regulated research and by the revised Common Rule (45 CFR 46, 2018 Requirements) for federally-funded research, now subject to a single-IRB mandate for cooperative research (Common Rule §46.114, compliance date Jan 20 2020; NIH sIRB policy effective Jan 25 2018) that pushes multi-site work toward central IRBs such as WCG and Advarra; (2) the DSMB/DMC, an independent body advising the sponsor on accumulating safety/efficacy data per FDA's 2006 guidance (being revised by a March 2024 draft, "Use of Data Monitoring Committees in Clinical Trials"), operating under a written charter; (3) clinical-trial monitoring, the sponsor's oversight of site conduct executed through a risk-based monitoring plan (SIV/IMV/COV visits) now governed by ICH E6(R3), which FDA adopted Sept 9 2025 and made effective, elevating risk-based quality management and "critical-to-quality" factors from option to expectation; and (4) pharmacovigilance/expedited safety reporting under 21 CFR 312.32, where a serious + unexpected + suspected adverse reaction (a SUSAR) triggers a 15-calendar-day IND safety report and a fatal/life-threatening one triggers a 7-calendar-day report, submitted narratively or on MedWatch FDA Form 3500A, rolled up annually under 21 CFR 312.33 (migrating to the ICH E2F DSUR). All four are stitched together by the ICH E6(R3) essential-records framework and the Trial Master File (TMF), the auditable evidentiary spine. For OSSICRO, every one of these entities has non-delegable human-judgment cores — IRB approval, DSMB recommendations, causality/expectedness determination, investigator/sponsor legal attestations — that software may assemble, route, time, and check, but must never author or own.

obligations_or_content.item: IRB review is mandatory before FDA-regulated human research begins
obligations_or_content.detail: No investigator may involve a human subject in FDA-regulated research unless the study has prior IRB review and approval; the IRB must be registered with FDA/OHRP. Applies to any clinical investigation of a drug/biologic under an IND. OSSICRO gates study start on documented IRB approval letter in the TMF.

obligations_or_content.item: IRB submission package contents
obligations_or_content.detail: Initial submission typically includes: protocol + amendments, investigator's brochure (IB), draft informed consent form(s) and any recruitment/advertising materials, investigator CV and medical license, Form FDA 1572 (statement of investigator), financial disclosure (21 CFR 54), IND number/sponsor authorization, HIPAA authorization, data-safety-monitoring plan, and site-specific context. OSSICRO generates/checks each as a template with a completeness validator.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 56.115; 21 CFR 50.25 (consent elements); 21 CFR 312.23 (IND contents referenced); Form FDA 1572; 21 CFR 54

obligations_or_content.item: Required elements of informed consent (IRB-approved, human-obtained)
obligations_or_content.detail: Eight basic elements (purpose, risks, benefits, alternatives, confidentiality, compensation/injury, contacts, voluntariness) plus additional elements when applicable, and the revised Common Rule 'key information' summary up front. Consent DOCUMENT is drafted by OSSICRO; the CONSENT EVENT (a qualified person obtaining voluntary agreement from the patient) is non-delegable to software.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 50.25(a)-(b); 21 CFR 50.20; 45 CFR 46.116 (incl. 46.116(a)(5) key-information)

obligations_or_content.item: IRB continuing review and expedited review
obligations_or_content.detail: Under FDA rules, IRB conducts continuing review at intervals appropriate to risk, at least once per year (21 CFR 56.109(f)). Revised Common Rule eliminated mandatory annual continuing review for certain minimal-risk/expedited and completed-except-for-analysis studies (45 CFR 46.109(f)(1)) unless the IRB documents a rationale. Amendments require IRB approval before implementation except to eliminate immediate hazards. OSSICRO tracks the continuing-review clock and generates the progress-report package.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 56.108-56.109; 21 CFR 56.110 (expedited categories); 45 CFR 46.109(f); 21 CFR 312.66

obligations_or_content.item: Single-IRB (sIRB) mandate for cooperative/multi-site research
obligations_or_content.detail: Revised Common Rule requires U.S. institutions engaged in cooperative research to rely on a single IRB (§46.114(b)), compliance date Jan 20 2020; NIH sIRB policy (NOT-OD-16-094) applies to multi-site NIH awards on/after Jan 25 2018. Exceptions where law requires more than sIRB or a federal agency documents sIRB is inappropriate. Drives use of central/independent IRBs (WCG IRB, Advarra) with reliance agreements. OSSICRO produces the reliance/authorization agreement and local-context forms.
obligations_or_content.citation: 45 CFR 46.114(b); NIH NOT-OD-16-094; NIH NOT-OD-20-058; FDA 21 CFR 56 permits (does not compel) central IRB use for FDA-regulated studies

obligations_or_content.item: Central/commercial IRBs (WCG, Advarra) as the sIRB of record
obligations_or_content.detail: WCG IRB and Advarra are the two dominant U.S. commercial central IRBs providing single-IRB-of-record service, master reliance agreements, and rapid review turnarounds for multi-site industry trials. They are the practical execution vehicle for the sIRB mandate and for physician-sponsor-investigators lacking an institutional IRB. OSSICRO integrates their submission checklists; the review DECISION remains the IRB's.
obligations_or_content.citation: Institutional: https://www.wcgclinical.com ; https://www.advarra.com ; operating under 21 CFR 56 / 45 CFR 46 authority

obligations_or_content.item: DSMB/DMC purpose and when recommended
obligations_or_content.detail: An independent Data Monitoring Committee reviews accumulating safety (and sometimes interim efficacy) data and advises the sponsor on whether to continue, modify, or stop a trial. FDA recommends a DMC especially for large, randomized, multi-site trials with mortality/major-morbidity endpoints, vulnerable populations, or high a-priori risk. Early-phase studies often use a simpler safety-review mechanism, but a formal DMC may still be warranted. The DMC advises; the sponsor decides and the IRB is informed.
obligations_or_content.citation: FDA 2006 Guidance 'Establishment and Operation of Clinical Trial Data Monitoring Committees'; superseding 2024 draft 'Use of Data Monitoring Committees in Clinical Trials' (Mar 2024); ICH E6(R3)

obligations_or_content.item: DMC charter contents
obligations_or_content.detail: A written charter should specify: DMC purpose/scope and possible recommendations; membership and roles; frequency/timing of meetings (organizational, open, closed sessions); statistical monitoring/interim-analysis plan and stopping guidelines; data flow to the DMC and the unblinded statistician; confidentiality and firewall procedures; conflict-of-interest policy; format of recommendations to the sponsor; and record-keeping/minutes. OSSICRO generates the charter template and validates required sections.
obligations_or_content.citation: FDA 2006 DMC Guidance §§ on charter; 2024 draft DMC guidance

obligations_or_content.item: DMC membership and independence
obligations_or_content.detail: Members must be free of significant financial, scientific, and regulatory conflicts of interest; typically 3+ members including clinicians in relevant specialties and at least one qualified biostatistician, plus an ethicist for higher-risk studies. Members may NOT be study investigators, employees of the sponsor, at the same institution as investigators, or from regulatory agencies. Prior DMC experience is valued. An independent (often unblinded) statistician supports the DMC outside the sponsor firewall.
obligations_or_content.citation: FDA 2006 DMC Guidance (membership/COI); 2024 draft DMC guidance

obligations_or_content.item: DMC meeting cadence and session structure
obligations_or_content.detail: Cadence set in the charter — commonly an organizational meeting pre-enrollment, then periodic reviews (e.g., every 3-6 months or after event/enrollment milestones), each with an open session (blinded, sponsor may attend) and a closed session (unblinded, DMC + independent statistician only). Output is a written recommendation and minutes to the sponsor. OSSICRO schedules the cadence and assembles the closed/open report packets (data prepared by the independent statistician, not the sponsor team).
obligations_or_content.citation: FDA 2006 DMC Guidance; 2024 draft DMC guidance

obligations_or_content.item: Clinical trial monitoring plan (sponsor obligation)
obligations_or_content.detail: The sponsor must monitor the conduct and progress of the trial to protect subjects and ensure data integrity, documented in a written, risk-based monitoring plan. E6(R3) requires the sponsor to identify critical-to-quality factors and design monitoring proportionate to risk (centralized + on-site mix). OSSICRO generates the monitoring plan template and tracks visit completion; the monitor (CRA) is a qualified human.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.50, 312.53(d), 312.56; ICH E6(R3) sponsor section (risk-based quality management, monitoring); FDA guidance 'A Risk-Based Approach to Monitoring of Clinical Investigations' (2013, updated 2023)

obligations_or_content.item: Monitoring visit types: SIV, IMV/routine, COV
obligations_or_content.detail: Site Initiation Visit (SIV) activates the site and confirms training/readiness before enrollment; Interim/Routine Monitoring Visits (IMV) verify consent, source-data (SDV where risk warrants), IP accountability, and protocol compliance during conduct; Close-Out Visit (COV) reconciles the site, drug accountability, and TMF completeness at end. Each produces a monitoring visit report filed to the TMF. OSSICRO generates visit-report templates and confirmation/follow-up letters.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) monitoring section; FDA 2013/2023 risk-based monitoring guidance; monitoring reports are essential records under E6(R3)

obligations_or_content.item: Risk-based / centralized monitoring under E6(R3)
obligations_or_content.detail: E6(R3) (FDA-adopted Sept 9 2025) elevates risk-proportionate monitoring: centralized/statistical monitoring and targeted on-site verification replace 100% source-data verification as default. Sponsors set quality tolerance limits and monitor critical data/processes. This is the interpretive shift OSSICRO's 'generate/check/validate' engine should encode — proportionality, not fixed checklists.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) Step 4 Final Guideline (2025-01-06); FDA adoption 90 FR / Fed. Reg. 2025-17311 (Sept 9 2025); EMA effective Jul 23 2025

obligations_or_content.item: IND safety reporting — 15-day expedited (SUSAR)
obligations_or_content.detail: Sponsor must report, as soon as possible but no later than 15 calendar days after determining it qualifies, any suspected adverse reaction that is BOTH serious AND unexpected (a SUSAR), plus certain findings from other studies, aggregate analyses showing the drug caused an event, and clinically important increased rate of serious suspected reactions. Causality and expectedness determinations are medical judgments (sponsor, often sponsor-investigator here) — non-delegable to software.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.32(c)(1)(i); definitions at 21 CFR 312.32(a); FDA final rule 75 FR 59935 (Sept 29 2010); FDA/investigator safety-reporting guidance (Dec 2012)

obligations_or_content.item: IND safety reporting — 7-day expedited (fatal/life-threatening)
obligations_or_content.detail: Any unexpected fatal or life-threatening suspected adverse reaction must be reported to FDA no later than 7 calendar days after the sponsor's initial receipt of the information, with a complete 15-day follow-up report thereafter. OSSICRO enforces the two clocks and routes the draft to the qualified reviewer; it does not adjudicate seriousness/causality.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.32(c)(2); follow-up under 312.32(c)(1) and (d)

obligations_or_content.item: Safety report format and MedWatch forms
obligations_or_content.detail: IND safety reports are submitted to FDA in narrative or on FDA Form 3500A (mandatory MedWatch, used by regulated industry/sponsors) or FDA-processable electronic format; the voluntary MedWatch form is FDA Form 3500. Sponsor must also notify all participating investigators of new safety information. Investigators report SAEs to the sponsor immediately per protocol and to the IRB (unanticipated problems) per IRB policy.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.32(c)-(d); FDA Form 3500A / Form 3500; investigator→IRB unanticipated-problem reporting per 21 CFR 56.108(b) and OHRP guidance

obligations_or_content.item: IND annual report / DSUR
obligations_or_content.detail: Sponsor must submit a brief annual report on the IND within 60 days of the IND anniversary (progress, safety summary, most-frequent/most-serious adverse reactions, IND amendments, IB changes, plans for next year). FDA accepts the ICH E2F Development Safety Update Report (DSUR) in lieu of the 312.33 annual report. OSSICRO assembles the DSUR/annual-report template from the accumulated safety line-listings.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.33; ICH E2F (DSUR); FDA guidance accepting DSUR for IND annual reporting

obligations_or_content.item: Essential records / Trial Master File (TMF) under E6(R3)
obligations_or_content.detail: E6(R3) reframes 'essential documents' as 'essential records' — the records that individually and collectively permit evaluation of trial conduct and data quality. Approach is risk-proportionate (adaptive matrix, not fixed list); sponsor and investigator must have direct access to all essential records regardless of format/location; retention deferred to applicable regulatory/contractual requirements (fixed 2-year retention language of E6(R2) removed). The TMF (sponsor) and Investigator Site File (site) are the two halves. OSSICRO IS effectively a TMF-assembly-and-completeness engine.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) essential-records annex; 21 CFR 312.57/312.62 (record retention); FDA E6(R3) adoption Sept 9 2025

obligations_or_content.item: Inter-entity document flows
obligations_or_content.detail: Protocol + IB flow from sponsor → IRB, DMC, and monitors. IRB approval letter + approved consent → sponsor/site/TMF, gating enrollment. SAE report flows investigator → sponsor → (assessed) → FDA (312.32) + all investigators + IRB (unanticipated problem) + DMC. DMC recommendation → sponsor → (if it changes risk) IRB + protocol amendment. Monitoring visit reports → sponsor/TMF; findings can trigger protocol deviations reported to IRB. Annual safety rollup (312.33/DSUR) → FDA + IRB + DMC. OSSICRO's coordination value is timing, routing, and completeness across exactly these arrows.
obligations_or_content.citation: Synthesizes 21 CFR 312.32-.33, .50-.56, .66; 21 CFR 56.108-.109; ICH E6(R3); FDA 2006/2024 DMC guidance

obligations_or_content.item: Sponsor-investigator dual-role obligations (OSSICRO's viable legal path)
obligations_or_content.detail: A sponsor-investigator holds BOTH sets of duties: sponsor duties (IND maintenance, safety reporting 312.32-.33, monitoring 312.50-.56, drug accountability, DMC establishment where warranted) AND investigator duties (protocol adherence, consent, 1572, SAE reporting to self-as-sponsor, IRB interface). CRO disintermediation is illegal because sponsor obligations transfer only to a legally accountable CRO entity (a person/organization), not software (21 CFR 312.52); OSSICRO instead reduces the coordination burden that makes the dual role impractical. The legal accountability stays with the physician.
obligations_or_content.citation: 21 CFR 312.3(b) (sponsor-investigator definition); 21 CFR 312.52 (transfer of obligations to a CRO); full sponsor duties 21 CFR 312 Subpart D; investigator duties Subpart D + 21 CFR 50/56
