institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Directly reusable as the seed for OSSICRO's IND-application generator — these are actual fillable FDA submission component templates, not summaries, and the amendment taxonomy (6 discrete amendment types) maps directly onto a document-state-machine design.

institutions.name: Northwestern University — NUCATS (Center for Clinical Research, Regulatory Unit)
institutions.resource_title: Regulatory Toolkit / Document Templates
institutions.what_it_offers: Templates grouped by lifecycle stage: recruitment/screening (screening-enrollment-withdrawal log, eligibility checklist, recruitment ad log, vulnerable-population worksheets), informed consent (ICF, consent-process documentation, assent forms, short-form + translations), study operations (CTA template, change-request checklists, CDA requests, protocol deviation log, drug/device accountability log, visit checklist, payment log), and regulatory binder components (DOA log).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Good source for the participant-facing / site-operations layer of the template library (distinct from Pitt/Penn's FDA-submission layer) — informed consent supplements and vulnerable-population checklists are directly relevant to the non-delegable-consent gating OSSICRO must enforce.

institutions.name: University of Michigan — MICHR (Michigan Institute for Clinical and Health Research)
institutions.resource_title: IND/IDE Investigator Assistance Program (MIAP)
institutions.type: sponsor-investigator IND guide + fee-based consultation service
institutions.what_it_offers: Structured consultation service (not a static toolkit): FDA regulatory-pathway determination, exemption-rationale development, FDA pre-IND meeting prep, IND/IDE application and protocol/consent development assistance, sponsor-investigator training, ongoing safety reporting and annual-report prep, protocol amendments, IND/IDE closeout. Free initial consult; $90/hr thereafter. Also offers a self-guided online IND/IDE Orientation Module course.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Best-documented process model (not template artifacts) for the human-consultation escalation path OSSICRO should route to when its automated pathway-determination logic is uncertain — a real precedent for a hybrid automated-triage + paid-expert-review model.

institutions.name: University of Pittsburgh — CTSI
institutions.resource_title: CTSI Regulatory Facilitators / Playbook (IND/IDE guides)
institutions.what_it_offers: Regulatory Facilitators embedded model — dedicated staff proactively guide investigators through required pathways; Playbook format breaks regulatory process into discrete guided steps.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Good structural precedent for OSSICRO's check/validate engine — the 'facilitator embedded at decision points' pattern maps to a rules-engine gate at each protocol milestone.

institutions.name: Yale — Yale Center for Clinical Investigation (YCCI)
institutions.resource_title: IND/IDE Application Support (Protocol Design, Development & Feasibility team)
institutions.what_it_offers: 'Soup to nuts' IND/IDE submission support staffed with ex-industry regulatory personnel; Clinical Trials Project Management team builds milestone-based regulatory timelines applicable to any funding source (NIH, pharma partnership, departmental).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Milestone/timeline-tracking model is a good template for OSSICRO's coordination-engine UI (a Gantt-like regulatory-milestone tracker across sponsor/IRB/DSMB/pharma counterparties).

institutions.name: Columbia University — Irving Institute for Clinical and Translational Research
institutions.resource_title: Plan Your Research — Ethics & Regulatory Consultation
institutions.what_it_offers: Ethics and regulatory consultations for investigators planning/implementing studies, including IRB submission assistance; works with 50+ partner medical institutions.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Low template yield; useful mainly as another instance confirming the CTSA-wide pattern of a dedicated regulatory-consult layer distinct from IRB itself — supports OSSICRO's design decision to separate 'ethics/regulatory-consult' from 'IRB-decision' as two different gated roles.

institutions.name: Washington University in St. Louis — ICTS Regulatory Support Center
institutions.resource_title: Regulatory Support / Trial-CARE
institutions.what_it_offers: Regulatory Core spans protocol/consent development, IND/IDE prep and maintenance, initial and ongoing IRB submissions, monitoring services for investigator-initiated studies, audit/inspection prep, and closeout.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Confirms the canonical full-lifecycle service scope (protocol→consent→IND/IDE→IRB→monitoring→closeout) that OSSICRO's workflow engine should mirror as its master phase list.

institutions.name: Vanderbilt — VICTR (Vanderbilt Institute for Clinical and Translational Research)
institutions.resource_title: Vanderbilt Customized Action Plan (V-CAP) / Program for Investigator Initiated Trials
institutions.what_it_offers: An informatics tool (published in PMC, see publications list) that asks structured questions about a proposed study and returns a printable, individualized list of required institutional approvals/applications with links to each application — up to 20 distinct approval types identified in their regulatory landscape assessment.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: This is the closest published precedent to OSSICRO's own 'generate/check/validate engine' concept — a rules-driven questionnaire that outputs a personalized regulatory checklist. Worth treating as prior art / design reference, not just a resource link.

institutions.name: UCLA — CTSI Office of Regulatory Affairs (Regulatory Knowledge and Support Program)
institutions.resource_title: Regulatory Knowledge and Support Program
institutions.what_it_offers: DSMB oversight, internal monitoring/audit support, FDA/sponsor inspection prep, ClinicalTrials.gov registration assistance, FDA IND/IDE guidance; stated program goal (in development) to build an 'FDA IND/IDE knowledge hub' and mHealth/digital-health regulatory support. Notably also references a CITI training course: 'Conducting Investigator-Initiated Studies According to FDA Regulations and GCP', which explicitly teaches the sponsor-investigator role and IND/IDE determination.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: The CITI course title/description is itself a useful curriculum outline for OSSICRO's onboarding/education module for enrolling clinicians who don't yet understand the sponsor-investigator role.

institutions.name: University of Washington / Seattle Children's / Fred Hutch — ITHS
institutions.resource_title: ITHS Research Services (WWAMI region)
institutions.what_it_offers: Regional (5-state WWAMI) service network offering DSMB services, recruitment support, multisite trial support, and early-stage drug/device development consulting; no discrete IND toolkit or template page found via this sweep.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Lower direct template yield in this pass; flag for a targeted follow-up search on iths.org for a dedicated regulatory-templates subpage before concluding no toolkit exists.

institutions.name: Emory University / Georgia CTSA
institutions.resource_title: Georgia CTSA Research Toolkit; Emory CTAC Investigator-Initiated Trials guide; Safety Reporting in IITs (course)
institutions.what_it_offers: A structured toolkit index (investigator-status guide, protocol support routing, IRB/IACUC/compliance-office links, clinical trials office routing) plus a Trial Innovation Network 'Navigator Team' that shepherds studies from concept through initial approval across Emory/Morehouse/UGA/Georgia Tech; a discrete continuing-ed course specifically on 'Safety Reporting in Investigator-Initiated Trials (IIT).'
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: The Navigator Team concept (single point of accountability walking a study concept to approval across multiple institutions) is a strong organizational model for OSSICRO's own coordination layer/micro-CRO function.

institutions.name: Mayo Clinic — Office of Research Regulatory Services (ORRS) / CCaTS
institutions.resource_title: IRB Reporting Requirements for Sponsor-Investigator (IDE); sponsor-investigator annual FDA report templates
institutions.what_it_offers: Institutional-policy-level document specifying exactly what a sponsor-investigator holding an IDE must report to the IRB and on what schedule; ORRS also assists with study-initiation-meeting agendas for sponsor-investigators.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Directly useful as a citable, published example of an institution's codified sponsor-investigator-to-IRB reporting-obligation schedule — a concrete rule set OSSICRO's validate-engine can encode (report type × trigger × deadline).

institutions.name: Weill Cornell Medicine — Clinical & Translational Science Center (CTSC), Regulatory and Ethics Knowledge Component (RKEC)
institutions.resource_title: DSMB Training Manual for Investigator-Initiated Studies (CTSA Collaborative DSMB Workgroup)
institutions.type: training manual / template guidance (freely downloadable PDF)
institutions.what_it_offers: NCATS-supported, cross-CTSA-network manual addressing the DSMB guidance gap specific to investigator-led (not commercially sponsored) studies. Written for PIs, DSMB members, IRB members, biostatisticians, and research staff. Editors: M.E. Blair Holbein, Barbara N. Hammack, Tasmin A. Knox, Laurel Yasko. Free PDF download, no login gate found.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Directly reusable as a source document for OSSICRO's DSMB/patient-safety-board module — it is a cross-institutional consensus product (not one institution's idiosyncratic policy), which strengthens its authority for a generalizable OSSICRO template.

institutions.name: NIH NCATS — CTSA Program hub / Trial Innovation Network (TIN)
institutions.resource_title: Trial Innovation Network — Central IRB / SMART IRB Reliance Platform / Standard Agreement System
institutions.what_it_offers: Network of all CTSA hubs; three Central IRBs (CIRBs) built on the SMART IRB Authorization Agreement; IRB Reliance Exchange (IREx) web platform operationalizing single-IRB review; draft SOPs/best-practices for the SMART IRB Reliance Platform (authorization + joinder agreements); a Standard Agreement System using the Federal Demonstration Partnership Clinical Trials Subaward Agreement (FDP-CTSA) as a harmonized multi-site contract template.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: The single most load-bearing find in this set for OSSICRO's IRB/ethics-committee coordination module: SMART IRB's authorization/joinder agreement structure and IREx platform are the real-world precedent for how OSSICRO should model IRB-of-record reliance when a sponsor-investigator's site is not its own IRB.

institutions.name: SOCRA / ACRP (professional bodies)
institutions.resource_title: Certification Exam Content Outlines (CCRP / CCRC / CCRA / CPI) and general SOP-category framework
institutions.type: training / SOP framework (no free standing public template library found)
institutions.what_it_offers: Professional certification content outlines that enumerate the knowledge domains (administrative, PI oversight, clinical management/participant safety, data management) expected of clinical research professionals; general guidance that sponsors maintain SOPs mapped to these categories, but no discrete open SOP template pack was located via this search pass.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Useful as a taxonomy check for OSSICRO's own SOP category scheme (admin / PI-oversight / safety / data-management) rather than as a source of reusable documents — the actual SOP libraries behind SOCRA/ACRP appear to be paywalled/member-only; flag for a follow-up search restricted to acrpnet.org and socra.org membership-resource pages specifically.

key_insights: Every CTSA hub converges on the same lifecycle taxonomy — protocol/consent development -> IND/IDE prep -> IRB submission -> study conduct/monitoring -> safety reporting -> closeout — which should become OSSICRO's canonical phase model rather than inventing a new one.

Two institutions have already built and PUBLISHED prior art directly analogous to OSSICRO's 'generate/check/validate engine': Vanderbilt's V-CAP (informatics tool that outputs a personalized required-approvals checklist from structured study-characteristic inputs) and NCATS/SMART IRB's IREx platform (operationalizes IRB-of-record reliance programmatically). Both are citable design precedents, not just resource links.

Penn (ITMAT/OCR) and Pitt (ECS-HSR) have the most complete, actually-downloadable IND/IDE template sets found across both search sets — Penn's numbered document-control scheme (e.g. 03.01.01, 05.02.07) is directly adoptable as OSSICRO's own template-numbering convention.

The DSMB Training Manual for Investigator-Initiated Studies is a cross-CTSA consensus product (not single-institution idiosyncratic policy) funded by NCATS — its multi-stakeholder authority (PI/DSMB/IRB/biostatistician audiences) makes it the strongest single source for OSSICRO's patient-safety-board module.

Mayo's codified sponsor-investigator-to-IRB reporting-obligation schedule (report type x trigger x deadline) is a concrete, encodable rule set for the validate-engine — most other institutions describe the service but don't publish the underlying rule table.

MICHR's fee-based ($90/hr post-free-consult) IND/IDE Investigator Assistance Program (MIAP) is useful precedent for OSSICRO's own 'thin micro-CRO operating layer' pricing/service-tier design: free automated triage, paid human escalation for genuinely hard regulatory-pathway determinations.

Several institutions (Columbia/ICAP, ITHS, SOCRA/ACRP) yielded service descriptions but not discrete public templates in this pass — likely because their concrete SOP/template libraries are internal-network-only or membership-gated; worth a targeted follow-up rather than treating absence-of-evidence as absence.

The FDA's own guidance document 'Investigational New Drug Applications Prepared and Submitted by Sponsor-Investigators' (fda.gov, PDF) surfaced independently in this sweep and should be cross-checked against Set 1 findings as it is likely the primary source several institutional toolkits (Penn, Pitt, UCLA) are operationalizing.

publications.citation: Holbein MEB. Understanding FDA regulatory requirements for investigational new drug applications for sponsor-investigators. J Investig Med. 2009 Aug;57(6):688-94.
publications.takeaway: According to PubMed, this review specifies the exact sponsor-investigator IND filing mechanics: three required forms (FDA Form 1571 study details, Form 1572 investigator/site info, Form 3674 ClinicalTrials.gov certification), the 30-day post-acknowledgment start clock, clinical-hold stop-work rule, and the ongoing sponsor-investigator monitoring/reporting obligations while the IND is active. Directly citable authority for OSSICRO's IND-application-generator form logic. DOI: https://doi.org/10.2310/JIM.0b013e3181afdb26

reusable_templates_found.name: Pitt ECS-HSR Initial IND Application Template (.doc) + full IND/IDE template set (protocol, CMC, pharm/tox, annual/safety/final reports, 6 amendment-type templates)
reusable_templates_found.source: University of Pittsburgh ECS-HSR
reusable_templates_found.license_or_terms: Institutional resource; terms of reuse outside Pitt not stated on page — verify permission before redistributing verbatim in OSSICRO, but structure/section-headings are standard FDA-derived content and safely reusable as a schema.

reusable_templates_found.name: Penn Medicine numbered document-control library (IND/IDE/aIDE application templates, IB template + addendum log, DSMP, PICA risk-tiered PI compliance assessment, TMF work-instructions)
reusable_templates_found.source: Penn Medicine Office of Clinical Research / ITMAT
reusable_templates_found.license_or_terms: Institutional resource; no explicit open license stated — treat as reference/schema source, confirm reuse terms before verbatim adoption.

reusable_templates_found.name: NUCATS Regulatory Toolkit template set (screening/enrollment log, ICF + supplements, CTA template, protocol deviation log, drug/device accountability log, DOA log)
reusable_templates_found.source: Northwestern NUCATS
reusable_templates_found.license_or_terms: Institutional resource, terms unstated; verify before reuse.

reusable_templates_found.name: DSMB Training Manual for Investigator-Initiated Studies
reusable_templates_found.source: CTSA Collaborative DSMB Workgroup (NCATS-supported), hosted by Weill Cornell CTSC
reusable_templates_found.license_or_terms: Free PDF download, cross-institutional consensus product; no explicit license stated but positioned as a public NCATS-funded network resource — best candidate among templates found for liberal reuse, still confirm before redistribution.

reusable_templates_found.name: Vanderbilt Customized Action Plan (V-CAP) methodology
reusable_templates_found.source: VICTR, published in PMC (PMC3767144)
reusable_templates_found.license_or_terms: Published academic article describing the tool's design (question set -> personalized approval checklist); the tool itself may not be publicly deployed outside Vanderbilt, but the published methodology is citable and reimplementable as an independent OSSICRO design.

reusable_templates_found.name: FDA guidance PDF: Investigational New Drug Applications Prepared and Submitted by Sponsor-Investigators
reusable_templates_found.source: U.S. FDA
reusable_templates_found.license_or_terms: U.S. Government work — public domain, freely reusable and directly citable as primary regulatory authority (should already be in OSSICRO's core citation set).

sweeps.scope: Set 2 institutional sweep for OSSICRO: Penn (ITMAT/CHPS), Yale (YCCI), Columbia (ICAP/Irving), Michigan (MICHR), WashU (ICTS), Vanderbilt (VICTR), UCLA (CTSI), UW (ITHS), Emory (Georgia CTSA), Mayo Clinic, Weill Cornell, Northwestern (NUCATS), Pittsburgh (CTSI), NIH NCATS/Trial Innovation Network, SOCRA, ACRP. Conducted via WebSearch + WebFetch + PubMed MCP.

institutions.name: FDA CDER/CBER
institutions.resource_title: IND Application Procedures: Investigator's Responsibilities
institutions.what_it_offers: Confirmed regulatory statement that a sponsor-investigator (21 CFR 312.3 definition: one who both initiates AND conducts an investigation, drug administered under their immediate direction) inherits BOTH investigator duties (312.60-312.69) and sponsor duties (312.50-312.59). Anchors the core legal architecture OSSICRO depends on.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Direct textual authority for the sponsor-investigator dual-role legal frame already established as OSSICRO's viable path; cite verbatim in the wiki's foundational legal-architecture page.

institutions.name: FDA CDER
institutions.resource_title: Investigator Responsibilities: Regulations and FDA Expectations
institutions.what_it_offers: FDA-authored slide/guidance deck detailing investigator obligations (informed consent, IRB approval maintenance, SAE reporting to sponsor, drug accountability, recordkeeping) mapped to specific 21 CFR 312 subsections.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Source for the OSSICRO 'obligation checklist' generator that validates a sponsor-investigator's document set against each named regulatory duty.

institutions.name: eCFR (National Archives)
institutions.resource_title: 21 CFR Part 312 Subpart D — Responsibilities of Sponsors and Investigators
institutions.what_it_offers: Live, canonical regulatory text (312.50-312.70): sponsor selection/monitoring of investigators, recordkeeping/record retention, informing investigators, review of ongoing investigations, general investigator responsibilities, investigator recordkeeping, investigator reports. This is the primary source of law, superior to any secondary summary.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: The single most load-bearing citation source for the entire regulatory-compliance engine; every generated document's validation rule should trace to a specific subsection here.

institutions.name: FDA
institutions.resource_title: Statement of Investigator (Form FDA 1572) + Instructions for Filling Out Form FDA 1572 + FAQ
institutions.what_it_offers: The canonical Form 1572 PDF, official completion instructions (fda.gov/media/79326/download), and an FDA FAQ page (fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/frequently-asked-questions-statement-investigator-form-fda-1572) covering CV/qualifications attachment, sub-investigator listing, IRB approval maintenance commitment, and per-co-investigator signature requirement.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Directly reusable as a generate/validate template — Form 1572 is a mandatory, structured, machine-parseable regulatory document; ideal first target for the document-generation engine given its fixed FDA schema.

institutions.name: TransCelerate BioPharma
institutions.resource_title: Common Protocol Template (CPT) — Clinical Content & Reuse Initiative
institutions.what_it_offers: Industry-consortium (19 member pharma companies incl. AstraZeneca) harmonized protocol template, now realigned to the finalized ICH M11 protocol template/technical specification and to CDISC's Unified Study Definitions Model (USDM). Available in a basic Word edition via a download-request form (free registration, no paywall found).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Best-available industry-standard protocol skeleton to seed OSSICRO's protocol-generation template — already pre-harmonized with the regulator-endorsed ICH M11 structure, reducing translation risk versus building from scratch.

institutions.name: TransCelerate BioPharma
institutions.resource_title: Statement of Investigator Form FDA 1572 Guidance (21 CFR Part 312, June 2015)
institutions.what_it_offers: TransCelerate's field-by-field completion guidance for Form 1572 cross-referenced to 21 CFR 312, mirrored on a third-party CRA-training site (myscrs.org).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Cross-check source for the Form 1572 validator's field-level rules; useful as a secondary interpretive authority alongside the FDA's own instructions.

institutions.name: ICH (International Council for Harmonisation)
institutions.resource_title: ICH E6(R3) Guideline for Good Clinical Practice (GCP) — Step 4 Final Guideline, adopted 6 Jan 2025
institutions.what_it_offers: The finalized, current GCP standard (superseding E6(R2)), with expanded content on quality-by-design, risk-based approaches to trial design/conduct/monitoring, data governance, and computerized systems. Also mirrored at EMA (https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/documents/scientific-guideline/ich-e6-r3-guideline-good-clinical-practice-gcp-step-5_en.pdf) and adopted by FDA (https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/e6r3-good-clinical-practice-gcp, Sept 2025).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Foundational GCP-compliance layer for the whole engine; the risk-based-monitoring and quality-by-design provisions directly inform OSSICRO's monitoring-plan generator and the essential-document / TMF checklist logic (E6(R3) restructured and expanded the Essential Documents annex relative to R2).

institutions.name: ICH (International Council for Harmonisation)
institutions.resource_title: ICH M11: Clinical Electronic Structured Harmonised Protocol (CeSHarP) — Guideline, Template, and Technical Specification, Final Template adopted 19 Nov 2025
institutions.what_it_offers: The first globally harmonized, regulator-adopted structured protocol template and machine-readable technical specification (headers, common text, data fields, controlled terminologies), applicable to all phases/therapeutic areas. FDA notice at federalregister.gov/documents/2025/06/06/2025-10359 and FDA guidance page (fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/m11-technical-specification-clinical-electronic-structured-harmonised-protocol); EMA mirror at ema.europa.eu/en/ich-m11-guideline-clinical-study-protocol-template-technical-specifications-scientific-guideline.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: THE canonical machine-structured protocol format for the generate/check/validate engine — CeSHarP's structured data-field model is exactly the schema OSSICRO's protocol generator should target for both human-readable output and regulator-facing electronic submission compatibility. Highest-priority template.

institutions.name: CTTI (Clinical Trials Transformation Initiative — Duke/FDA co-founded)
institutions.resource_title: Quality by Design (QbD) Toolkit, Principles Document, Metrics Framework, Maturity Model, Implementation Guide
institutions.what_it_offers: Practical operationalization of 'errors that matter' quality-by-design principle for trial design/conduct, built by an FDA-co-founded public-private partnership. Includes a get-started toolkit page (ctti-clinicaltrials.org/about/ctti-projects/quality-by-design/qbd-quality-by-design-toolkit/get-started/).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Direct source for the monitoring-plan/risk-assessment module logic; CTTI recommendations are widely cited by FDA guidance and are a defensible, non-proprietary basis for OSSICRO's risk-based-monitoring check engine.

institutions.name: NIH (Office of Science Policy) + FDA
institutions.resource_title: NIH-FDA Clinical Trial Protocol Template for Phase 2/3 IND/IDE Studies (v1.07, Aug 2018) + Electronic Protocol Writing Tool
institutions.what_it_offers: Government-developed (not industry) protocol template built specifically for NIH-funded Phase 2/3 trials under an IND/IDE, with a companion collaborative electronic-writing tool. Mirrored on ClinicalTrials.gov (cdn.clinicaltrials.gov/large-docs/11/NCT03658811/Prot_SAP_000.pdf) and described at NIH OSP (osp.od.nih.gov/in-perfect-harmony-the-nih-fda-clinical-trials-protocol-template/).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: A public-sector (non-consortium, no registration wall) alternative/complement to TransCelerate's CPT — valuable because it is fully open and government-authored, avoiding any licensing ambiguity for an open-source project. Good default protocol skeleton for the sponsor-investigator (rather than large-pharma-sponsor) use case.

institutions.name: NIH institutes (NIDDK / NIAID / NIEHS / NIA / NIAMS / NIDCR)
institutions.resource_title: Sample Data and Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) Charter templates + DSMB policy documents
institutions.what_it_offers: Multiple NIH-institute-specific sample DSMB charters (NIDDK; NIA at nia.nih.gov/sites/default/files/2023-03/sample_data_and_safety_monitoring_board_dsmb_charter.docx; NIEHS at niehs.nih.gov/research/atniehs/labs/crb/ohrce/dsmb/charter) plus NIAID's formal DSMB policy (niaid.nih.gov/sites/default/files/dsmbpolicyv5.pdf) specifying when a DSMB is mandatory (multisite trials with participant risk) and charter content requirements (charge, responsibilities, operating rules, open/closed session structure).
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Directly reusable, freely available government templates for the DSMB/patient-safety-board module — one of OSSICRO's six named coordinating entities. Multiple charter variants let the generate engine offer a study-risk-tiered charter (single-site low-risk vs multisite high-risk).

institutions.name: NCATS (National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences)
institutions.resource_title: NCATS Toolkit — 'Prepare for Clinical Trials' and 'Clinical Trials and FDA Review' modules
institutions.what_it_offers: Structured, plain-language modules on sponsor role definition (a sponsor-investigator is explicitly named as a sponsor who also conducts the study), site readiness, FDA review process, and participant recruitment/education strategies, oriented toward patient-advocacy-group and academic investigator audiences rather than industry.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Good source for OSSICRO's clinician-facing (non-regulatory-expert) explanatory layer — the plain-language register needed for a neurologist enrolling a patient, distinct from the CFR/ICH primary-source layer.

institutions.name: Harvard Catalyst (Harvard Clinical and Translational Science Center)
institutions.resource_title: IND/IDE Consult Service — decentralized network model for sponsor-investigator support
institutions.what_it_offers: Published (peer-reviewed) description of an operating academic-medical-center consult service that gives sponsor-investigators and IRBs shared regulatory expertise across a multi-institution network, explicitly because 'investigator sponsors often do not fully appreciate their regulatory obligations nor have resources to ensure compliance.'
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Empirical validation of OSSICRO's core thesis (the sponsor-investigator role is impractical without absorbed coordination support) and a real-world precedent for the 'thin legally-accountable layer + shared infrastructure' operating model OSSICRO proposes to implement in software rather than a consult service.

institutions.name: Advarra
institutions.resource_title: IRB Handbook for Investigators, Institutions, Sponsors, and Sponsors' Representatives (v6, July 2024) + CIRBI platform
institutions.what_it_offers: A commercial central-IRB's published handbook orienting investigators/sponsors to submission requirements, initial review through study closeout, and reliance-agreement mechanics. Advarra's actual consent-form and submission templates sit behind the CIRBI platform (cirbi.net), which requires registration and is not freely downloadable — no open-license templates found from Advarra itself.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Useful as a reference for how a commercial central-IRB structures its submission workflow (informs OSSICRO's IRB-coordination module UX), but NOT a source of reusable open templates — flag this distinction; Advarra's actual document templates are proprietary/access-gated.

institutions.name: FDA
institutions.resource_title: E6(R3) Good Clinical Practice (GCP) guidance page
institutions.what_it_offers: FDA's own adoption notice/page for ICH E6(R3), confirming US regulatory applicability (Sept 2025) alongside the ICH-international text.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Confirms E6(R3) is FDA-recognized guidance, not merely an international aspirational document — needed to justify citing E6(R3) as authoritative in a US-frame wiki.

institutions.name: ClinicalTrials.gov (NLM/NIH)
institutions.resource_title: ClinicalTrials.gov Data and API (v2)
institutions.what_it_offers: Official v2 REST API for programmatic search/retrieval of registered trials, with documented phase enum values (PHASE1, PHASE2, PHASE3, PHASE4, EARLY_PHASE1, NA) and status enum values (RECRUITING, ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING, etc.). Free, no registration wall found for basic API use.
institutions.usefulness_to_ossicro: Direct integration target for OSSICRO's 'searchable database of early-phase pharma programs' — the guiding-scenario requirement of matching a patient to an open Phase 2 program can be built directly on this API rather than a scraped or licensed dataset. Noted limitation: no semantic/synonym matching, so OSSICRO's own matching layer needs to add that.

key_insights: The sponsor-investigator legal frame is textually confirmed at the primary-source level: 21 CFR 312.3 defines a sponsor-investigator as one who both initiates AND conducts a study with the drug under their immediate direction, and FDA's own IND-procedures page states this role inherits both investigator duties (312.60-312.69) and sponsor duties (312.50-312.59) simultaneously -- no software/CRO disintermediation path exists in the text; the dual-hat human role is the only mechanism.

Form FDA 1572 is the single most tractable first target for OSSICRO's generate/check/validate engine: it is a fixed FDA-published PDF schema with official field-by-field completion instructions (fda.gov/media/79326) and an FDA FAQ page, plus a secondary TransCelerate interpretive guidance doc -- multiple independent sources to cross-validate generator logic against.

ICH M11 (final template adopted 19 Nov 2025) is a genuinely new development: it is the first regulator-endorsed, machine-readable structured protocol format (CeSHarP) with defined data fields and controlled terminology, harmonized with CDISC's Unified Study Definitions Model. This is a materially better target schema for OSSICRO's protocol generator than free-text templates, because it's built for electronic exchange rather than just document authoring -- prioritize this over TransCelerate's CPT even though CPT is now realigned to it.

ICH E6(R3) (final, adopted 6 Jan 2025, FDA-recognized Sept 2025) meaningfully restructured GCP relative to R2: it embeds quality-by-design and risk-based-monitoring as core principles rather than an add-on, and expanded data-governance/computerized-systems content. OSSICRO's risk-based monitoring-plan generator should be built against R3, not the older R2 text still cited in many secondary sources.

There is a public-sector alternative to every major industry (TransCelerate) resource: NIH-FDA's own Phase 2/3 protocol template, NIH institutes' DSMB charter templates (NIDDK/NIAID/NIEHS/NIA/NIAMS), and NCATS's toolkit are all free, non-consortium-gated, and government-authored -- these are the safer open-source base layer for OSSICRO versus TransCelerate assets, which require a download-request/registration gate and are consortium (large-pharma-member) intellectual property with unclear onward-redistribution terms for an open-source project.

Advarra's actual reusable templates (consent forms, submission forms) sit behind the gated CIRBI platform (cirbi.net) and are not openly licensed -- Advarra is useful only as a reference for commercial-central-IRB workflow structure, not as a source of forkable open templates. This distinction should be flagged explicitly in the wiki so contributors don't assume Advarra material is reusable.

Empirical literature on investigator-initiated trials is concentrated in low/middle-income-country and academic-medical-center contexts (Harvard Catalyst IND/IDE Consult Service; Cameroon CAMPS trial; developing-country systematic review), consistently identifying the same failure mode OSSICRO targets: investigator-sponsors lack regulatory-compliance resources and institutional capacity, driving both non-compliance risk and outright trial abandonment. This is the strongest peer-reviewed evidentiary support for the project's premise.

ClinicalTrials.gov's v2 API is a viable, free, no-registration integration point for the 'searchable database of early-phase pharma programs' guiding scenario, but it explicitly lacks semantic/synonym matching (per its own documentation) -- OSSICRO's patient-to-trial matching layer must add its own semantic layer on top rather than relying on ClinicalTrials.gov's native search.

Decentralized clinical trial (DCT) literature (FDA Oncology Center of Excellence / CTTI joint COVID-era survey of 13 sponsors across 52 trials, and the ASA Biopharmaceutical Section's 2025 statistical-perspective paper) shows regulatory restrictions and institutional policy were named barriers by 56% and 83% of sponsors respectively even at large pharma companies -- for a sponsor-investigator with far fewer resources, these coordination burdens are proportionally much larger, reinforcing the case for OSSICRO's coordination-automation layer specifically around DCT-adjacent elements (remote monitoring, telemedicine, remote drug distribution).

publications.citation: Kim MJ, Winkler SJ, Bierer BE, Wolf D. Harvard Catalyst | The Clinical Translational Science Center IND/IDE Consult Service: providing an IND/IDE consult service in a decentralized network of academic healthcare centers. Clin Transl Sci. 2014;7(2):150-5.
publications.takeaway: Direct empirical precedent for OSSICRO's thesis: sponsor-investigators lack regulatory-compliance resources, and a shared cross-institutional support layer (here, a human consult service; OSSICRO proposes software) meaningfully reduces that burden.

publications.citation: Mbuagbaw L, Thabane L, Ongolo-Zogo P, Lang T. The challenges and opportunities of conducting a clinical trial in a low resource setting: the case of the Cameroon mobile phone SMS (CAMPS) trial, an investigator initiated trial. Trials. 2011;12:145.
publications.takeaway: Concrete, itemized administrative/ethical/financial/staffing difficulties encountered in a real investigator-initiated trial, with the authors proposing a documentation checklist to ease the organizational process -- directly maps onto OSSICRO's document-coordination scope.

publications.citation: Alemayehu C, Mitchell G, Nikles J. Barriers for conducting clinical trials in developing countries- a systematic review. Int J Equity Health. 2018;17(1):37.
publications.takeaway: Systematic review identifying five unifying barrier themes (financial/human capacity, ethical/regulatory obstacles, research environment, operational barriers, competing demands) and explicitly recommending wider implementation of local investigator-initiated trials as the remedy -- supports OSSICRO's premise at the systematic-review evidence tier.

publications.citation: Patel TH, Corneli A, Balcazar P, Lipset C, Calvert SB, Mervin-Blake S, Nalawade V, Kluetz PG. Adoption of Decentralized Trial Elements in Cancer Clinical Trials Supporting FDA Approvals during COVID-19. Clin Cancer Res. 2025;31(10):1827-1830.
publications.takeaway: FDA Oncology Center of Excellence / CTTI joint survey of 13 sponsors across 52 FDA-approval-supporting trials: 95% adopted at least one DCT element, but institutional policy (83%) and regulatory restrictions (56%) were the top implementation barriers even among large sponsors -- quantifies the coordination burden OSSICRO targets.

publications.citation: Chen J, Di J, Daizadeh N, Lu Y, Wang H, Shen YL, Kirk J, Rockhold FW, Pang H, Zhao J, He W, Potter A, Lee H. Decentralized Clinical Trials in the Era of Real-World Evidence: A Statistical Perspective. Clin Transl Sci. 2025;18(2):e70117.
publications.takeaway: ASA Biopharmaceutical Section working-group paper (with FDA co-authors) summarizing current DCT regulatory guidance/framework and design/analysis/safety-reporting implications -- useful for OSSICRO's decentralized-element compliance-checking logic.

publications.citation: Green SS, Lee SJ, Chahin S, Pooler-Burgess M, Green-Jones M, Gurung S, Outlaw AY, Naar S. Regulatory Issues in Electronic Health Records for Adolescent HIV Research: Strategies and Lessons Learned. JMIR Form Res. 2024;8:e46420.
publications.takeaway: Empirical multisite-trial account: regulatory/reliance-agreement setup spanned 6-10 months across 8 sites even with a phased approach -- concrete timeline data point for OSSICRO's site-activation-timeline documentation and for setting realistic user expectations in the frontend.

publications.citation: Takaoka A, Johnstone J, Lauzier F, Heels-Ansdell D, Davis M, Zytaruk N, Duan E, Dionne J, Saunders L, Arabi YM, Marshall J, Thabane L, Clarke F, Hand L, Masse MH, Rochwerg B, McIntyre L, Girard M, Freitag A, Karachi T, Cook DJ. Enrolment patterns in a randomized controlled trial of probiotics in critically ill patients: a retrospective analysis of the PROSPECT trial. Trials. 2024;25(1):851.
publications.takeaway: 41-site multicenter RCT analysis: early site launch (not coordinator experience or time-to-first-enrollment) predicted higher enrollment rate -- relevant to OSSICRO's site-activation sequencing/prioritization logic, and a caution that experience/training alone doesn't solve enrollment velocity.

sweeps.scope: Exhaustive sweep across (1) PubMed peer-reviewed literature on sponsor-investigator IND process, investigator-initiated trial conduct/challenges, site activation timelines, decentralized trials, protocol/eligibility feasibility, essential-document/TMF management, and risk-based monitoring; (2) publicly published CRO/industry resources (TransCelerate, Advarra, CTTI); (3) FDA/NIH/ICH regulatory toolkits and templates (Form 1572, ICH M11, ICH E6(R3), NIH-FDA protocol template, NIH DSMB charters, ClinicalTrials.gov API, NCATS toolkit). US-frame primary per project context (21 CFR 312, FDA guidances), with ICH/EU noted where load-bearing. All items below are cited with URL and, for publications, PMID/DOI.
